topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick, suffocatingly sweet with perfume and forced merriment. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute, the crystal cold against my palm, watching the happy couple laugh across the crowded floor. Then, a familiar silhouette cut through the periphery of my vision—him. He was standing near the buffet, looking impossibly relaxed, his smile wide and utterly effortless. My breath hitched, a tiny, involuntary catch in my chest. Every muscle in my jaw tightened, a silent refusal to move, to acknowledge the magnetic pull that yanked me toward him like a tide. I forced my gaze down to the intricate pattern of the carpet, focusing intently on the threadbare weave, trying to anchor myself to something solid. He turned slightly, and for a fraction of a second, our eyes met. The recognition was sharp, a sudden, unwelcome jolt of memory that made my carefully constructed composure wobble precariously. I took a deliberate, slow sip of my drink, the ice clinking too loudly in the sudden, deafening silence inside my own head.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be mingling, a social lubricant, but I found myself pressed against a marble pillar, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole. The noise of the celebration was a relentless assault, each burst of laughter echoing hollowly against the vast space. Then, there he was, across the room, leaning against a doorway, talking animatedly to someone I vaguely recognized. The conversation seemed to wrap around him like a protective bubble, and I could only watch, a spectator in my own life. A strange heaviness settled in my shoulders, a leaden weight that made rising feel like an insurmountable physical effort. My throat constricted. I wanted desperately to retreat, to melt into the ornate drapery, but my feet remained rooted to the polished floor. When he finally glanced over, catching my eye for a fleeting second, a wave of profound inertia washed over me. I quickly looked away, my cheeks burning hot, and focused instead on the distant, glittering chandeliers, trying to will the feeling away.

[story 3]

The music vibrated through the floorboards, a relentless, upbeat rhythm that seemed determined to pull me along, but I felt disconnected, adrift in the current. I was standing near the edge of the terrace, nursing a glass of water, observing the scene with a detached curiosity that bordered on painful. Then, he appeared. It was like a spotlight had been unexpectedly switched on, illuminating everything around him while leaving me in a muted shadow. The years seemed to compress into that single moment; I could feel the familiar, unwelcome clench in my stomach, a tightening that made my hands tremble slightly. I wanted to pivot, to walk toward the exit, but my legs felt cemented to the spot. Every instinct screamed at me to run, to disappear into the periphery, yet I remained frozen, observing the interaction from a distance, trapped in a loop of recognition and profound, unresolved stillness.
